hero.title: Documentation
hero.subtitle: Everything you need to build reactive web apps with No.JS

introduction.title: Introduction
introduction.text: No.JS is an HTML-first reactive framework. Build dynamic, data-driven web applications using nothing but HTML attributes — no build step, no virtual DOM, no JSX.
introduction.callout: ~24KB gzipped · Zero dependencies · Works in all modern browsers · No build step required

installation.title: Installation
installation.selfHostedText: Download dist/iife/no.js and include it with a <script> tag. It's a single file, ~24 KB gzipped.

quickStart.title: Quick Start
quickStart.text: Create an index.html file: include the script, add some attributes, and you're done. No app.mount(), no createApp(), no NgModule. It just works.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.text: On DOMContentLoaded, No.JS walks the DOM looking for elements with known attributes. Each attribute maps to a directive that is executed by priority.
howItWorks.card1Desc: Walks the DOM looking for elements with known attributes.
howItWorks.card2Desc: Each attribute maps to a directive executed by priority (data fetching first, then conditionals, then rendering).
howItWorks.card3Desc: All data lives in reactive contexts (Proxy-backed). When data changes, every bound element updates automatically.
howItWorks.card4Desc: Contexts inherit from parent elements, like lexical scoping. A bind inside an each loop can access both the loop item and ancestor data.

coreConcepts.title: Core Concepts
coreConcepts.reactiveContextText: Every element can have a context — a reactive data object. Contexts are created by state, get, store, etc. Child elements inherit their parent's context automatically.
coreConcepts.expressionText: Most directive values accept JavaScript expressions evaluated against the current context:

hero.title: Directive Cheatsheet
hero.subtitle: Complete reference of every No.JS directive

data.title: Data
data.base: Set API base URL for descendants
data.as: Name for fetched data in context
data.into: Write response to a named global store
data.success: Template ID to show on success

state.title: State
state.persist: Attribute of state directive — persists state to storage

loops.title: Loops
loops.sort: Sort property (name only, not prefixed with item variable)

events.title: Events
events.self: Only fires if event target is the element itself

routing.title: Routing
routing.routeActiveExact: Exact match active class for route links
routing.lazyPriority: Load remote template before all others (Phase 0)
routing.lazyOnDemand: Fetch route template only when first visited (route templates only)
routing.routeMatched: true if an explicit route matched, false for wildcard/fallback
routing.i18nNs: Auto-derive i18n namespace from route filename

dnd.title: Drag and Drop
dnd.dragGroup: Scope drag to a named group
dnd.dropSettleClass: Custom CSS class for settle animation
dnd.dropEmptyClass: Custom CSS class for empty state on drag-list
dnd.dragList: Sortable list bound to state array
dnd.dragListCopy: Copy instead of move on transfer
dnd.dragListRemove: Remove items from source on transfer

misc.title: Misc
misc.loading: Placeholder shown while remote template loads; removed on arrival
misc.include: Synchronously clone an inline template into the current position

hero.title: State Management
hero.subtitle: Local state, global stores, computed properties, and watchers

state.title: state — Local State
state.text: Creates a reactive context scoped to the element and its children.

store.title: store — Global Store
store.text: A global reactive store accessible from anywhere. Ideal for auth state, theme, shared data.

configStores.title: Pre-initializing Stores via config()
configStores.text: You can pre-create stores with initial data inside NoJS.config(). Each key in the stores object becomes a named global store, accessible immediately via $store.name.
configStores.callout: If a store already exists (e.g. created via a store attribute), config() will not overwrite it — the existing store is preserved.

into.title: into — Write Fetch Results to a Store
into.text: The into attribute on any HTTP directive writes the response directly into a named global store.
into.callout: The store doesn't need to be pre-defined — into will create it if it doesn't exist.

computed.title: computed — Derived State
computed.text: Values that are automatically recalculated when dependencies change.

watch.title: watch — Side Effects
watch.text: Execute an action whenever a value changes.

persistence.title: State Persistence
persistence.text: Persist state across page reloads using localStorage or sessionStorage.

notify.title: NoJS.notify() — Flush Store Updates
notify.text: When external JavaScript mutates a store via NoJS.store, call NoJS.notify() to flush all pending DOM updates.
notify.callout: Only needed when mutating NoJS.store from plain JavaScript — outside of HTML expressions. Store mutations inside on:click or bind expressions are handled automatically.

hero.title: Data Binding
hero.subtitle: One-way and two-way data binding with bind and model

bind.title: bind — Text Content
bind.text: Replaces the element's textContent with the evaluated expression.

bindHtml.title: bind-html — Inner HTML
bindHtml.text: Renders evaluated expression as HTML. Sanitized by default.
bindHtml.callout: ⚠️ Uses built-in regex-based sanitization: strips <script> tags, blocks on* event handlers, and removes javascript: URIs.

model.title: model — Two-Way Binding
model.text: For form inputs, model creates automatic two-way data binding.

hero.title: Event Handling
hero.subtitle: Bind DOM events directly in HTML with on:event syntax

handlers.title: on:* — Event Handlers
handlers.text: Bind any DOM event directly in HTML. Access state and context variables directly in the handler expression.

modifiers.title: Event Modifiers
modifiers.text: Modifiers let you control event behavior directly in the attribute:

eventAndEl.title: $event & $el
eventAndEl.text: $event is the native DOM event. $el refers to the current element.

hero.title: Conditionals
hero.subtitle: Control rendering with if, show, hide, and switch

ifThenElse.title: if / then / else
ifThenElse.text: Conditionally render elements or templates based on expressions.

showHide.title: show / hide
showHide.text: Toggles display: none without adding/removing DOM elements. Better for frequently toggled elements.

switchCase.title: switch / case
switchCase.text: Render one of many templates based on a value.

hero.title: Loops
hero.subtitle: Iterate over arrays with each and foreach

foreach.title: foreach — Extended Loop
foreach.text: Offers more control with filtering, sorting, pagination, and custom variable names.
foreach.key: Unique key expression for element identification and tracking
foreach.else: Template ID to render when array is empty
foreach.sort: Property path to sort by (prefix - for descending)
foreach.limit: Maximum number of items to render

nested.title: Nested Loops
nested.text: Child loops can access parent scope variables.

hero.title: Templates
hero.subtitle: Reusable HTML fragments with variables, slots, and remote loading

basic.title: Basic Template
basic.text: Templates are reusable HTML fragments that are never rendered directly. They are cloned when referenced by directives like then, else, template, loading, error, etc.

var.title: Template Variables (var)
var.text: Templates can declare which variable they expect from the calling context.

slots.title: Template Slots
slots.text: Allow templates to accept projected content.

remote.title: Remote Templates (src)
remote.text: Load templates from external HTML files.

recursive.subtitle: Recursive Loading
recursive.text: Remote templates are loaded recursively — if a remote template itself contains <template src="..."> elements, those are automatically resolved too:

remoteRoutes.subtitle: Remote Templates in Routes
remoteRoutes.text: Remote templates inside route content are also automatically resolved before the route renders. See Routing for details.

lazy.title: Lazy Loading (lazy)
lazy.text: Control when remote templates are fetched using the lazy attribute on <template src="..."> elements. NoJS loads templates in phases to optimise time-to-first-render.
lazy.absentDesc: Default auto-prioritisation: content-include templates and the current route template load before first render; other route templates are preloaded in the background after first render.
lazy.priorityDesc: Force load before everything else — even before regular content includes. Useful for critical shared layout templates.
lazy.ondemandDesc: Only valid on route templates. Never preloaded — fetched lazily the first time the user navigates to that route. Ideal for heavy or rarely-visited pages.

phases.subtitle: Loading Phases
phases.text: Templates are resolved in four ordered phases: Phase 0 fetches lazy="priority" templates first; Phase 1 fetches all other non-route templates plus the active route template (blocking before first render); Phase 2 preloads remaining route templates in the background after first render; and on-demand fetches lazy="ondemand" route templates only when the user first navigates to them.

loading.title: Loading Placeholder (loading)
loading.text1: Show a placeholder template while a remote template is being fetched. The placeholder is inserted synchronously — before any network request — and removed automatically once the real content arrives. Works for both static content-includes and nested templates inside route pages.
loading.text2: Both plain IDs and #id syntax are accepted. The placeholder template is cloned each time, so it can be reused across multiple remote templates:

include.title: Inline Template Include (include)
include.text1: Clone an inline template into the current position synchronously, before any fetches. Useful for injecting reusable markup (e.g. icon sets, common fragments) without making a network request.
include.text2: include and loading serve different purposes: include clones inline content permanently; loading inserts a temporary placeholder that disappears once a remote template finishes loading.

hero.title: Data Fetching
hero.subtitle: Declarative HTTP requests — just add attributes to HTML elements

baseUrl.title: Base URL
baseUrl.text1: Set once on any ancestor element. All descendant get, post, etc. resolve relative URLs against it.

get.title: get — Fetch and Render Data
get.as: Name to assign the response in the context. Default: "data"
get.loading: Template ID to show while loading (e.g. "#skeleton")
get.error: Template ID to show on fetch error
get.empty: Template ID to show when response is empty array/null
get.cached: Cache responses. cached = memory, cached="local" = localStorage, cached="session" = sessionStorage
get.into: Write response to a named global store
get.debounce: Debounce in ms (useful with reactive URLs)
get.params: Expression that resolves to query params object

reactiveUrls.title: Reactive URLs
reactiveUrls.text: URLs that reference state variables re-fetch automatically when those values change.

mutations.title: post, put, patch, delete — Mutating Requests
mutations.text: Used on forms or triggered via call.

mutationAttrs.title: Mutation Attributes
mutationAttrs.body: Request body (JSON string with interpolation). For forms, auto-serializes fields
mutationAttrs.success: Template ID to render on success. Receives response as var
mutationAttrs.error: Template ID to render on error. Receives error as var
mutationAttrs.loading: Template ID to show during request
mutationAttrs.confirm: Show browser confirm() dialog before sending
mutationAttrs.redirect: URL to navigate to on success (SPA route)
mutationAttrs.into: Write response to a named global store
mutationAttrs.cached: Cache responses (memory/local/session). Note: caching only applies to GET requests.

hero.title: Routing
hero.subtitle: Full client-side SPA navigation with no page reloads

context.title: $route — Route Context
context.matched: Whether an explicit route matched (true) or a wildcard/fallback is rendering (false)

programmatic.title: Programmatic Navigation
programmatic.callout: $router.push() and $router.replace() return Promises — navigation (including remote template loading) is fully async. In on:click handlers the return value is ignored, but in scripts you can await them:

remoteTemplates.title: Remote Templates in Routes
remoteTemplates.text1: Route templates can include <template src="..."> to load content from external files. They are automatically resolved before the route renders:
remoteTemplates.text2: Nested remote templates (a remote template that itself contains more <template src>) are recursively loaded.

fileBased.title: File-Based Routing
fileBased.text: Instead of declaring each route template manually, point your route-view outlet at a folder. No.JS will automatically resolve route paths to template files inside that folder.
fileBased.list1: Add route-view to your outlet element — file-based routing is enabled by default (config router.templates: "pages"). Override per-outlet with src="folder/".
fileBased.list2: When a user navigates to /analytics, No.JS resolves it to pages/analytics.tpl
fileBased.list3: The template is fetched, cached, and rendered — automatically
fileBased.srcDesc: Base folder for template resolution (per-outlet override; config: router.templates)
fileBased.extDesc: File extension appended to route segments (fallback: ".html")
fileBased.i18nNsDesc: When present, auto-derives i18n namespace from filename
fileBased.callout: Config default: The default router.templates is "pages", so file-based routing works out of the box — just add route-view to your outlet. Override with NoJS.config({ router: { templates: 'views' } }) or per-outlet via src="./custom/".
fileBased.exampleText: That's it — two lines for a full SPA with six routes.
fileBased.mixingText: Explicit <template route="..."> declarations always take priority. This lets you combine both approaches — use file-based routing for simple pages and explicit templates for routes that need guards, params, or named outlets:
fileBased.autoI18nText: When the route-view element has an i18n-ns attribute (even without a value), No.JS automatically loads the i18n namespace matching the filename:
fileBased.autoI18nText2: This replaces the need to add i18n-ns="..." on each route template individually.

lazyLoading.title: Lazy Template Loading
lazyLoading.text: The lazy attribute on <template src="..."> controls when a remote template is fetched relative to the first render. Use it to prioritise critical templates and defer heavy or rarely-visited pages.
lazyLoading.absentDesc: Auto: non-route templates and the active route template load before first render (Phase 1); other route templates preload in the background after first render (Phase 2).
lazyLoading.priorityDesc: Load before everything else — even before regular content includes. Use for critical shared layout templates.
lazyLoading.ondemandDesc: Only valid on route templates. Never preloaded — fetched the first time the user navigates to that route. Ideal for heavy or rarely-visited pages.

anchor.title: Anchor Links
anchor.text1: When using useHash: true, the URL hash (#) is used for routing (e.g. #/docs). This normally conflicts with standard anchor links like <a href="#section"> — but No.JS handles it automatically in both hash and history modes.
anchor.text2: Anchor links that point to an element id on the page are intercepted by the router: the target element is scrolled into view smoothly, and the clicked link receives an active class. The route itself is not affected.
anchor.list1: Clicking <a href="#introduction"> scrolls to <div id="introduction"> with smooth behavior
anchor.list2: The .active class is toggled on the clicked link (and removed from siblings)
anchor.list3: The current route path is preserved — no navigation occurs
anchor.list4: Links with a route attribute are always treated as route navigation, not anchors
anchor.tip: Tip: Style the active anchor link with .active in your CSS — the router manages the class for you.

namedOutlets.title: Named Outlets (route-view)
namedOutlets.text: Multiple route-view outlets can coexist in the same page. Give each outlet a name via the attribute value, and point route templates at specific outlets using the outlet attribute.
namedOutlets.callout: Outlets with no matching template for the current route are always cleared on navigation.

catchAll.title: 404 / Catch-All Routes
catchAll.text: Use route="*" to define a wildcard catch-all template that renders when no explicit route matches the current path. The wildcard is always evaluated last, regardless of DOM order.
catchAll.text2: Explicit routes always take priority — the wildcard only fires when matchRoute() returns no match.
catchAll.fallbackText: If you don't define a route="*" template, No.JS automatically shows a minimal built-in 404 page when no route matches. This ensures users always see something meaningful instead of a blank outlet.
catchAll.fallbackTip: The built-in fallback is intentionally minimal and unstyled. Define your own route="*" template for production apps.
catchAll.namedText: Each named outlet can have its own wildcard fallback. When no route matches for an outlet, the framework resolves fallbacks in this order:
catchAll.namedList1: Local wildcard — <template route="*" outlet="{name}"> for that specific outlet
catchAll.namedList2: Global wildcard — <template route="*"> (the default outlet's wildcard), used only for non-default outlets
catchAll.namedText2: If the sidebar has no local wildcard, it falls back to the global route="*". If neither exists, the built-in 404 is used.
catchAll.matchedText: The $route.matched boolean tells you whether the current path hit an explicit route (true) or a wildcard/fallback (false). Use it for conditional rendering inside your templates:
catchAll.matchedText2: $route.matched is set before the template renders, so it's always available during processing.
catchAll.remoteText: Wildcard routes support all the same attributes as regular route templates, including src for remote loading:
catchAll.remoteText2: The remote template is fetched, cached, and rendered just like any other route template — and it has full access to $route.path, $route.matched, and all other framework features.
catchAll.fileBasedText: When using file-based routing, navigating to a path whose .tpl file doesn't exist on the server (HTTP 404 or other error) automatically triggers the wildcard fallback chain.
catchAll.fileBasedText2: The failed HTTP response is not cached — subsequent navigations to other paths are unaffected.

hero.title: Forms & Validation
hero.subtitle: Declarative form submission with built-in and custom validation rules

perRuleErrors.title: Per-Rule Error Messages
perRuleErrors.text: Use error-{rule} attributes to set a custom message for a specific rule, or error for a generic fallback.

errorTemplates.title: Error Templates
errorTemplates.text: Point an error attribute to a <template> using a # prefix to render rich error UI. Inside the template, $error contains the message and $rule the failing rule name.

errorClass.title: Error CSS Class
errorClass.text: Use error-class on the form or on individual fields to toggle a CSS class when a field is invalid and touched.

formContext.title: $form — Form Context
formContext.text: Inside any <form> with the validate attribute, $form provides:
formContext.valid: true if all fields pass validation
formContext.dirty: true if any field has been modified
formContext.touched: true if any field has been focused and blurred
formContext.submitting: true while the request is in flight
formContext.pending: true while async validators are running
formContext.errors: Map of field names → error messages
formContext.firstError: Error message of the first invalid field (DOM order)
formContext.errorCount: Number of fields currently failing validation
formContext.fields: Per-field state object (valid, error, dirty, touched)
formContext.reset: Reset form to initial values, clear errors and classes

formFields.title: $form.fields — Per-Field State
formFields.text: $form.fields exposes individual field state keyed by field name.
formFields.asText: Use the as attribute to expose a field's state under a custom name in the context.

validateOn.title: Validation Triggers (validate-on)
validateOn.text: By default, validation runs on input and focusout. Use validate-on to change when visual feedback appears.
validateOn.note: Internally, $form data is always kept up-to-date regardless of validate-on. The trigger only controls when visual feedback is shown.

validateIf.title: Conditional Validation (validate-if)
validateIf.text: Skip validation for a field based on a condition. When validate-if evaluates to false, the field is treated as valid.

autoDisable.title: Auto-Disable Submit Buttons
autoDisable.text: Submit buttons are automatically disabled when the form is invalid. Buttons with type="button" are not affected.

hero.title: Dynamic Styling
hero.subtitle: Toggle CSS classes and inline styles reactively

hero.title: Animations & Transitions
hero.subtitle: Declarative enter/leave animations and CSS transitions

transition.title: transition — CSS Transition Classes
transition.text1: Follows a convention similar to Vue's transition system.
transition.text2: No.JS adds/removes classes during the transition:

loopAnimations.title: Loop Animations
loopAnimations.text: Both each and foreach loops support enter/leave animations and stagger.

builtIn.title: Built-in Animation Names
builtIn.text: No.JS ships with these CSS animations:

hero.title: Drag and Drop
hero.subtitle: Declarative drag, drop, sortable lists and multi-select — zero JavaScript

drag.title: drag — Make an Element Draggable
drag.dragTypeDesc: Named type — only matching drop-accept zones respond
drag.dragHandleDesc: Restricts grab area to a child element
drag.dragImageOffsetDesc: Pixel offset for custom drag image
drag.dragGhostClassDesc: Class added to the drag image element

drop.title: drop — Define a Drop Zone
drop.dropClassDesc: Class added when valid item hovers
drop.dropRejectClassDesc: Class added when item is rejected (wrong type or max exceeded)
drop.dropSettleClassDesc: Custom CSS class for the settle animation
drop.dropEmptyClassDesc: Custom CSS class for empty state on drop zone

dragList.title: drag-list — Sortable List
dragList.text: A higher-level directive that combines drag and drop into a sortable list bound to a state array.
dragList.dragListKeyDesc: Unique key per item for stable identity
dragList.dropSettleClassDesc: Custom CSS class for the settle animation
dragList.dropEmptyClassDesc: Custom CSS class for empty state on drag-list
dragList.dropPlaceholderDesc: Shows a placeholder where the item will be dropped

dragMultiple.title: drag-multiple — Multi-Select
dragMultiple.text: Enable click-to-select on child elements, then drag all selected items at once.
dragMultiple.demoText: Click items to select, <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+click for multi, then drag to the drop zone:
dragMultiple.dragGroupDesc: Group name — all selected items move together
dragMultiple.selDragResult: $drag becomes an array of all selected items

implicitVars.title: Implicit Variables
implicitVars.text: These variables are available inside drop expressions and on:drop handlers:
implicitVars.dragDesc: The dragged value. Array if multi-select
implicitVars.dropIndexDesc: Insertion index within the drop zone

cssClasses.title: CSS Classes
cssClasses.draggingDesc: On the source element while dragging
cssClasses.dragOverDesc: On the drop zone while a valid item hovers
cssClasses.dropRejectDesc: On the drop zone when the item is rejected (wrong type or max exceeded)
cssClasses.dragListEmptyDesc: On a drag-list when it has no items
cssClasses.demoText: Drag an item and watch the classes apply:

a11y.title: Accessibility
a11y.text: No.JS automatically adds ARIA attributes and keyboard support:
a11y.demoText: Use <kbd>Tab</kbd> to focus, <kbd>Space</kbd> to grab, <kbd>↑↓</kbd> to move, <kbd>Enter</kbd> to drop:

hero.title: Filters & Pipes
hero.subtitle: Transform values in bind expressions using the | pipe syntax

hero.title: Internationalization (i18n)
hero.subtitle: Multi-language support with translations, pluralization, and locale-aware formatting

externalFiles.title: External Locale Files
externalFiles.text: Instead of inlining all translations in JavaScript, you can load them from external JSON files. This is ideal for large apps with many locales or when translations are managed by a separate tool.
externalFiles.nsText: Split translations by feature for code-splitting and on-demand loading:
externalFiles.nsRouteText: Use i18n-ns on a route template to load a namespace on-demand when the route is navigated to:
externalFiles.nsElementText: Use i18n-ns on any element to load a namespace before its children are processed:
externalFiles.cachingText: Fetched JSON files are cached in memory by default. Set cache: false during development:

hero.title: Actions & Refs
hero.subtitle: Trigger API calls, emit custom events, and reference DOM elements

call.title: call — Trigger API Requests from Any Element

refsMap.title: $refs — Ref Map
refsMap.text: All elements with ref are accessible via $refs in the current scope:

hero.title: Custom Directives
hero.subtitle: Extend No.JS with your own attribute-driven behaviors

webComponents.title: Web Components Compatibility
webComponents.text: No.JS directives work on custom elements:

hero.title: Error Handling
hero.subtitle: Per-element error templates, retry logic, and global error handlers

hero.title: Configuration & Security
hero.subtitle: Global settings, request interceptors, and security best practices

configStores.title: Pre-initializing Stores
configStores.text: Use the stores key in NoJS.config() to pre-create named global stores with initial data. Each entry becomes a reactive store accessible via $store.name anywhere in your markup.
configStores.callout: Stores created via config() will not overwrite stores that already exist — the first definition wins.

configOptions.title: Config Option Details
configOptions.sanitizeText: Controls whether HTML content rendered via bind-html is sanitized through a DOMPurify-compatible sanitizer. When enabled, all potentially dangerous tags and attributes (e.g., <script>, onerror) are stripped before insertion into the DOM.
configOptions.devtoolsText: Enables the No.JS devtools panel, accessible via window.__NOJS_DEVTOOLS__. When active, it exposes reactive state, registered directives, active routes, and component trees for inspection in the browser console.
configOptions.cspText: Set to 'strict' to disable the use of new Function() for expression evaluation. This is required for environments with a Content Security Policy that blocks unsafe-eval. In strict mode, expressions are limited to dot-path property access and simple comparisons — arbitrary JavaScript is not allowed.
configOptions.templatesCacheText1: Controls whether the HTML content of remotely-fetched .tpl files is stored in an in-memory Map after the first request. On repeated navigations to the same route, the cached HTML is used directly and no HTTP request is made. The cache lives for the duration of the page session (no TTL — template assets are static).
configOptions.templatesCacheText2: Set to false during local development if you want changes to .tpl files to be reflected without a hard page reload.
configOptions.loadPathText: URL template for loading locale JSON files via fetch. Use {locale} and optionally {ns} as placeholders. When null, translations must be provided inline via NoJS.i18n({ locales }).
configOptions.nsText: Array of namespace identifiers to preload at init(). Each namespace corresponds to a separate JSON file per locale. Additional namespaces can be loaded on-demand via the i18n-ns directive or route attribute.
configOptions.cacheText: Controls whether fetched locale JSON files are stored in an in-memory Map after the first request. Set to false during development for hot-reload of translation files.

apiProperties.title: API Properties
apiProperties.baseApiUrlText: Getter/setter for the base API URL used by all fetch directives and NoJS.http calls. Can be read or reassigned at runtime.
apiProperties.versionText: Read-only property that returns the current No.JS framework version string.

security.title: Security
security.xssList1: bind always sets textContent, never innerHTML — safe by default.
security.xssList2: bind-html sanitizes content using a built-in regex-based sanitizer (strips <script> tags, blocks on* event handlers, removes javascript: URIs).
security.xssList3: Template expressions are evaluated in a sandboxed Function() scope — no access to window, document, or globals unless explicitly exposed.
security.cspSecText1: No.JS uses new Function() for expression evaluation. If your CSP blocks unsafe-eval, use the precompiled mode:
security.cspSecText2: In strict mode, expressions are limited to dot-path access and simple comparisons (no arbitrary JS).
